section.title: Cities of Refuge
section.sectionRationale: As the tribes prepare to settle their allotted land, God provides for the Levites, who receive no territory of their own, and establishes six cities of refuge where someone who kills unintentionally can flee from the avenger of blood. This law holds together real justice for murder and real mercy for the unintentional, a pattern later Scripture reads as a picture of refuge found in Christ.
section.thesis: The Levites receive forty-eight cities with pasturelands instead of tribal territory, six of which are set apart as cities of refuge where a person who kills someone unintentionally may flee for protection from the avenger of blood, safe there until the death of the high priest, while a deliberate murderer finds no such shelter and must be put to death.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the Levites given cities and pasturelands instead of land; six of those cities set apart as places of refuge; the sharp legal distinction between murder and unintentional killing; and the rules of testimony, ransom, and bloodguilt that protect the land's holiness.

verses.text: Again the LORD spoke to Moses on the plains of Moab by the Jordan across from Jericho:
“Command the Israelites to give, from the inheritance they will possess, cities for the Levites to live in and pasturelands around the cities.
The cities will be for them to live in, and the pasturelands will be for their herds, their flocks, and all their other livestock.
The pasturelands around the cities you are to give the Levites will extend a thousand cubits from the wall on every side.

You are also to measure two thousand cubits outside the city on the east, two thousand on the south, two thousand on the west, and two thousand on the north, with the city in the center. These areas will serve as larger pasturelands for the cities.
Six of the cities you give the Levites are to be appointed as cities of {{refuge|refuge}}, to which a manslayer may flee. In addition to these, give the Levites forty-two other cities.
The total number of cities you give the Levites will be forty-eight, with their corresponding pasturelands.

The cities that you apportion from the territory of the Israelites should be given to the Levites in proportion to the inheritance of each tribe: more from a larger tribe and less from a smaller one.”

media.alt: A city gate standing open on a hillside road as evening falls
media.caption: A refuge from the avenger, until the death of the high priest.

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses,
“Speak to the Israelites and tell them: When you cross the Jordan into the land of Canaan,
designate cities to serve as your cities of refuge, so that a person who kills someone unintentionally may flee there.
You are to have these cities as a refuge from the {{avenger|avenger of blood}}, so that the manslayer will not die until he stands trial before the assembly.
The cities you select will be your six cities of refuge.
Select three cities across the Jordan and three in the land of Canaan as cities of refuge.
These six cities will serve as a refuge for the Israelites and for the foreigner or stranger among them, so that anyone who kills a person unintentionally may flee there.

units.label: Murder distinguished from manslaughter
units.note: the murderer must surely be put to death

verses.text: If, however, anyone strikes a person with an iron object and kills him, he is a murderer; the murderer must surely be put to death.
Or if anyone has in his hand a stone of deadly size, and he strikes and kills another, he is a murderer; the murderer must surely be put to death.
If anyone has in his hand a deadly object of wood, and he strikes and kills another, he is a murderer; the murderer must surely be put to death.
The avenger of blood is to put the murderer to death; when he finds him, he is to kill him.
Likewise, if anyone maliciously pushes another or intentionally throws an object at him and kills him,
or if in hostility he strikes him with his hand and he dies, the one who struck him must surely be put to death; he is a murderer. When the avenger of blood finds the murderer, he is to kill him.
But if anyone pushes a person suddenly, without hostility, or throws an object at him unintentionally,
or without looking drops a heavy stone that kills him, but he was not an enemy and did not intend to harm him,
then the congregation must judge between the slayer and the avenger of blood according to these ordinances.

The assembly is to protect the manslayer from the hand of the avenger of blood. Then the assembly will return him to the city of refuge to which he fled, and he must live there until the death of the high priest, who was anointed with the holy oil.

units.label: Testimony, ransom, and the holiness of the land

verses.text: But if the manslayer ever goes outside the limits of the city of refuge to which he fled
and the avenger of blood finds him outside of his city of refuge and kills him, then the avenger will not be guilty of bloodshed
because the manslayer must remain in his city of refuge until the death of the high priest. Only after the death of the high priest may he return to the land he owns.
This will be a statutory ordinance for you for the generations to come, wherever you live.
If anyone kills a person, the murderer is to be put to death on the testimony of the witnesses. But no one is to be put to death based on the testimony of a lone witness.
You are not to accept a ransom for the life of a murderer who deserves to die; he must surely be put to death.
Nor should you accept a ransom for the person who flees to a city of refuge and allow him to return and live on his own land before the death of the high priest.
Do not pollute the land where you live, for bloodshed pollutes the land, and no atonement can be made for the land on which the blood is shed, except by the blood of the one who shed it.
Do not defile the land where you live and where I dwell. For I, the LORD, dwell among the Israelites.”

refuge.gloss: Refuge, a place of shelter. A miqlat city was not a hiding place from justice but a place of due process, protecting the unintentional killer from vengeance until a fair trial determined guilt, a real, physical picture of Scripture's larger theme of finding refuge from deserved judgment in God Himself.

avenger.gloss: Avenger of blood, literally kinsman-redeemer of blood, the near relative responsible for pursuing justice for a slain family member. The same word, go'el, is elsewhere used positively of a redeemer who buys back a relative's loss, showing that justice and redemption in Hebrew thought were carried by kin rather than distant institutions alone.

basic.context: The Levites, who receive no tribal territory of their own, are given forty-eight cities scattered throughout the land, six of which are set apart as cities of refuge. Anyone who kills another person unintentionally can flee there for protection from the victim's avenging relative, safe until a fair trial, and safe within the city's borders until the current high priest dies. A deliberate murderer, by contrast, finds no such shelter.
basic.hermeneutics: This is real, practical law establishing due process and mercy within Israel's justice system, carefully distinguishing between murder and accident. Its precision, defining weapons, intent, and witnesses, shows a legal system determined to protect both true justice for murder and genuine mercy for those who kill without intending harm.
basic.originalLanguages: A miqlat, city of refuge, was not an escape from justice but a place where due process could unfold safely, protecting a person from the go'el hadam, avenger of blood, until guilt or innocence was properly established. The same root go'el elsewhere describes a kinsman-redeemer who buys back what was lost, linking blood justice and redemption to the same family responsibility.
basic.typology: The cities of refuge, offering real safety from deserved judgment to anyone who reaches them, and holding that safety only until the death of the high priest, are widely read as a picture of refuge found in Christ, our great High Priest, whose own death secures permanent freedom for all who flee to Him, Hebrews 6:18.
basic.christ: Hebrews 6:18 speaks of believers who have fled for refuge to take hold of the hope set before them, language many read as directly echoing this very institution; and where the manslayer's freedom in these ancient cities came only through the death of an earthly high priest, believers' freedom comes through the death of Jesus, our eternal High Priest, whose one sacrifice secures release once for all.
basic.god: God is both perfectly just, requiring the death penalty for deliberate murder and refusing any ransom to buy off that judgment, and perfectly merciful, providing real, accessible, physical shelter for those who kill without murderous intent. His holiness will not let the land bear unaddressed bloodguilt, yet His compassion ensures the innocent are not swept away with the guilty.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 19:1-13 and Joshua 20 give further instructions and the actual naming of these six cities. Hebrews 6:18 describes believers fleeing for refuge to the hope set before them, language closely echoing this institution. Proverbs 18:10 pictures the name of the LORD as a strong tower where the righteous run and are safe.
basic.oneStory: Real justice for the guilty and real mercy for the innocent are held together in this law rather than set against each other. That same combination, holy justice and genuine refuge, runs through the whole Bible and finds its fullest expression in Christ, the true and eternal High Priest, whose death secures permanent, unshakeable safety for everyone who flees to Him in faith.

context.literary: This law closes out the section of Numbers dealing with the land's coming allotment, addressing both the Levites' unique landless status and the justice system that must operate once Israel is settled across the entire territory rather than gathered in a single wilderness camp.
context.historical: Six cities of refuge, three on each side of the Jordan, ensured that no Israelite would ever be more than a short journey from a place of safety and fair trial, a remarkably practical provision for a nation about to spread across an entire land rather than remain centrally camped.
context.canonical: Joshua 20 records the actual naming and establishment of these six cities, and the concept of refuge from deserved judgment becomes a recurring biblical image, developed further in the Psalms and ultimately applied directly to believers' hope in Christ in Hebrews.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish a justice system that protects both the community's need for real justice against murder and the individual's need for protection and due process when a death occurs without murderous intent.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This chapter is specific judicial law for ancient Israel's land-based justice system; it does not prescribe an identical legal or civic structure for believers or nations today. Its abiding principle, holding together real justice and real mercy, and its picture of refuge from judgment, carries forward, fulfilled in Christ rather than replicated in physical cities.

debates.question: Why does the manslayer's safety depend specifically on the death of the high priest rather than a fixed sentence?

views.label: The high priest's death functioned as a kind of atoning, community-wide release
views.case: Since the high priest represented the whole nation before God, his death may have functioned similarly to a jubilee release, marking a point at which the bloodguilt-related restriction on the manslayer's movement was lifted for the community as a whole, tied to the priesthood's representative, atoning role.

views.label: It simply provided an unpredictable, impartial endpoint not subject to manipulation
views.case: Tying release to an event outside anyone's control or prediction, rather than a fixed number of years, prevented both the manslayer and the avenger from calculating or manipulating the timing of release, keeping the provision impartial and outside human control.

debates.bearing: Both readings converge on why later interpreters find this detail so strikingly typological: release from confinement came only through a priestly death, not through the passage of time or personal merit, a pattern Hebrews 6 and 9 pick up directly in describing the release secured for believers through the death of Jesus, our great High Priest.

study.originalLanguages: A miqlat, refuge, protected due process rather than evading justice, holding the manslayer safe from the go'el hadam, avenger of blood, until a fair hearing determined the truth. That the same go'el root describes a kinsman-redeemer elsewhere in Scripture shows that both vengeance and redemption were understood as family responsibilities in ancient Israel's legal imagination.

typology.type: Cities of refuge offering real safety from deserved vengeance to anyone who reaches them
typology.fulfillment: Widely read as a picture of the refuge believers find in Christ, who is Himself described as a shelter and hope to those who flee to Him, Hebrews 6:18.

typology.type: Release from confinement secured by the death of the high priest
typology.fulfillment: Foreshadows the release from bondage secured once for all through the death of Jesus, our eternal and greater High Priest, Hebrews 9:11-12.

study.christ: Hebrews 6:18 describes believers as those who have fled for refuge to lay hold of the hope set before them, language that closely echoes this very institution of cities of refuge. Where the ancient manslayer's freedom depended on the death of Israel's earthly high priest, believers' freedom from condemnation is secured permanently through the death of Jesus, our great and eternal High Priest, whose single sacrifice, unlike the repeated succession of Israel's priests, never needs to be repeated or waited out again, Hebrews 9:11-12, 25-26.
study.god: God's holiness will not overlook bloodguilt on the land, refusing any ransom that would let deliberate murder go unpunished; yet His mercy provides real, accessible, physical safety for anyone who kills without murderous intent, ensuring that justice and compassion are never set against each other in His law.

crossReferences.note: Gives further detailed instructions for establishing and using the cities of refuge.
Records the actual naming and establishment of the six cities of refuge in the land.
Describes believers fleeing for refuge to the hope set before them, language closely echoing this institution.
Pictures the name of the LORD as a strong tower where the righteous run and find safety.

study.oneStory: God's law holds together real justice for the guilty and real mercy for the innocent, refusing to let either collapse into the other. This pattern of holy justice paired with accessible refuge runs through the whole Bible and comes to its fullest expression in Christ, our eternal High Priest, whose own death secures lasting safety for everyone who flees to Him in faith.

quiz.q: How many cities in total were given to the Levites, and how many of those were cities of refuge?

options.text: Forty-eight cities total, six of them cities of refuge.
options.note: Verses 6 to 7 state both numbers exactly.

options.text: Twelve cities total, all of them cities of refuge.
options.note: The text specifies forty-eight total cities, with only six designated specifically as refuge cities.

options.text: Six cities total, given entirely as refuge cities.
options.note: Six is the correct number of refuge cities specifically, but the Levites received forty-two additional cities beyond these six.

options.text: No specific numbers are given.
options.note: Verses 6 to 7 state precise numbers.

quiz.q: What was the purpose of the cities of refuge, according to verses 11 to 12?

options.text: To protect a person who killed someone unintentionally from the avenger of blood until a fair trial.
options.note: Verses 11 to 12 state this purpose exactly.

options.text: To provide permanent housing for retired priests.
options.note: Their stated purpose is protection for the unintentional killer, not priestly retirement housing.

options.text: To shelter deliberate murderers from any punishment at all.
options.note: The text specifically distinguishes unintentional killing from murder, and deliberate murderers received no such protection.

options.text: No purpose is given for these cities.
options.note: Verses 11 to 12 state the purpose clearly.

quiz.q: What determined how long the manslayer had to remain within the city of refuge, according to verses 25 and 28?

options.text: He had to remain until the death of the high priest.
options.note: Both verses state this condition directly.

options.text: He had to remain for exactly one full year.
options.note: The text ties his release to the high priest's death, not a fixed one-year term.

options.text: He could leave at any time once the victim's family agreed.
options.note: The text specifies no such family-based release; it is tied strictly to the high priest's death.

options.text: No release condition is mentioned.
options.note: Both verses state the condition explicitly.

quiz.q: What happened if the manslayer left the boundaries of his city of refuge before the high priest's death, according to verses 26 to 27?

options.text: If the avenger of blood found and killed him outside the city, the avenger would not be guilty of bloodshed.
options.note: Verses 26 to 27 state this exact consequence.

options.text: He would simply be escorted back to the city with no other consequence.
options.note: The text specifies real danger and legal consequence if he left the protected boundary, not a simple, safe escort back.

options.text: He would automatically be granted permanent freedom.
options.note: Leaving the city's protection actually placed him at risk, not granted him freedom.

options.text: No consequence for leaving the city is mentioned.
options.note: Verses 26 to 27 describe the consequence in detail.

quiz.q: According to verse 31, could a murderer pay a ransom to avoid the death penalty?

options.text: No, a ransom was explicitly forbidden for a murderer who deserved to die.
options.note: Verse 31 states this prohibition directly.

options.text: Yes, if the victim's family agreed to accept payment.
options.note: The text explicitly forbids any ransom for a convicted murderer, regardless of family agreement.

options.text: Yes, but only if the murderer was wealthy enough.
options.note: No such wealth-based exception exists; the prohibition applies without exception.

options.text: No mention of ransom is made in this chapter.
options.note: Verse 31 addresses ransom explicitly.

questions: The cities of refuge offered real safety, but only for those who fled to them honestly seeking protection. Where in your life do you need to actively run toward the safety God offers rather than passively hoping for protection?

The manslayer's freedom depended entirely on someone else's death, the high priest's, not on his own effort or the passage of time alone. How does resting in what Christ has already accomplished, rather than your own effort, change how you approach guilt or fear of judgment?

This law refused to let murder go unpunished for any price, yet provided genuine mercy for the truly unintentional. Where do you need to hold together taking sin seriously and still extending real mercy to those who have genuinely failed without malicious intent?
The land itself was said to be polluted by unaddressed bloodguilt. Where might unresolved wrongdoing be quietly affecting more of your life or community than you realize?

Six cities meant no Israelite was ever far from a place of refuge. How does knowing that Christ, our refuge, is always near and always accessible change how quickly you turn to Him in a moment of guilt or fear?

translationNotes.note: The two measurements given for Levitical pastureland, a thousand cubits in verse 4 and two thousand cubits in verse 5, are usually harmonized as describing an inner wall boundary and an outer field boundary respectively, rather than a contradiction in the text.

Avenger of blood renders go'el hadam, using the same Hebrew root, go'el, elsewhere translated kinsman-redeemer, showing that both vengeance for the slain and redemption of the lost were understood in ancient Israel as responsibilities carried within the family line.

No atonement can be made for the land on which blood is shed, except by the blood of the one who shed it reflects the seriousness with which the Torah treats bloodguilt as a real defilement of the land itself, not merely a matter between individuals.
